high_consensus.description: Clear ownership gap on a decision that directly affects later implementation shape. The added gate is low-cost, aligns with the stated operator pair-point pattern, and prevents avoidable S3 rework.

Strong regression protection for a load-bearing contract. The specific concern that OTEL wrapping mutates text may be lower probability, but byte-identical telemetry-only behavior is important enough to test after the wrapper is introduced.
Valuable low-cost config hardening. Pinning env semantics prevents operator confusion and keeps behavior consistent with the existing prose-gate parsing pattern.
Important because misconfiguration can break Discord delivery despite default-case tests passing. Explicit bounds and warnings are cheap relative to the runtime failure they prevent.
High ROI baseline capture. It is cheap, improves regression claims across multiple sprints, and removes ambiguity when later failures appear.
Good deterministic-spec fix with modest implementation cost. The tie case may be uncommon, but deterministic attribution matters for tests, telemetry, and operator trust.

disputed.description: Very low-cost documentation and configuration hygiene with practical operator benefit. Consolidating env vars in .env.example should be part of the cycle close.
Worth doing because the LoC budget is used as a gate, but the impact is mostly process determinism rather than runtime correctness. Low effort keeps it above nice-to-have.

blockers.concern: Discord 1024-char embed field limit mitigation relies on a static count limit (19 factors) instead of dynamic character counting. Long factor names combined with emojis and separators can easily exceed 1024 chars even with fewer than 19 factors.
blockers.why_matters: If the rendered string exceeds 1024 characters, the Discord API will reject the payload with a 400 Bad Request, causing the weekly digest delivery to fail entirely.
blockers.recommendation: Implement a dynamic truncation algorithm in `buildPulseDimensionPayload` that accumulates the character length of each rendered row and truncates with the `…and N more silent` token when the next row would breach the 1024 limit.

blockers.concern: Prose-gate factor attribution has conflicting fallback behavior and can create noisy or misleading violations.
blockers.why_matters: T1.3 says no factor context emits no-factor-context, while the risk section says no exact match runs checks against all factors. That can flag generic header or outro prose because any unrelated factor fails a substrate check, which will pollute telemetry and make future soft-enforcement unsafe.
blockers.recommendation: Choose one fallback rule and test it explicitly. Prefer: if no referenced factor can be attributed, emit a bounded no-factor-context telemetry item without applying mechanical factor-specific reasons.

blockers.concern: The HARD score-mibera precondition can silently downgrade to a warning when /health returns an unknown schema version.
blockers.why_matters: The plan says score-mibera 1.1.0 in prod is a hard blocker, but the verifier permits proceeding when the endpoint returns '?'. That can let S1-S4 build against absent or stale factor_stats and produce false confidence.
blockers.recommendation: Make unknown schema version a hard failure unless a named manual override file or explicit operator approval is recorded in S0-COMPLETED.md with evidence from a live factor_stats payload.

blockers.concern: The S0 precondition script uses `$MCP_KEY` and `$SCORE_API_URL` for the HARD-2 health check without verifying if these environment variables are actually set in the environment.
blockers.why_matters: If the variables are missing, the curl command fails silently, falls back to `?`, and produces a warning instead of a hard failure. This effectively bypasses the HARD precondition validation and masks local configuration errors.
blockers.recommendation: Add explicit exit-on-empty validation for `$MCP_KEY` and `$SCORE_API_URL` at the start of the bash script before attempting the network call.
